svg.title: How a standard Random Forest combines many decision trees
svg.desc: A three-step diagram of a standard Random Forest. Each tree receives a bootstrap sample containing n complete training-row draws with replacement. At every eligible split, a fresh random subset of features is offered and the best split among those candidates is selected. A new case is passed through each tree. Classification uses the class receiving the most votes, while regression averages numerical predictions. Three schematic trees are shown for clarity; real forests contain many much deeper trees.

text.tspan: A standard Random Forest builds many diverse decision trees and combines
their predictions to reduce variance and improve stability.
For each tree, draw n complete training rows
with replacement from the n-row data set.

svg.text: Each symbol is a complete row; color shows class.

text.tspan: Rows can repeat; unselected rows are out-of-
bagfor that tree. Out-of-bag predictions can
estimate predictive error without a separate
At every eligible node, offer a fresh random
feature subset and choose its best split.

g.text: Orange = offered; dark outline = chosen best split.

diversify the trees and reduce correlated

Random Forest combines many trees built from bootstrap row samples
and fresh random feature subsets at each split.

svg.text: Averaging diverse trees usually reduces prediction variance and improves stability.

text.tspan: Classification: class with the most votes
Sampling options can vary by implementation.
